privacy_note: Derived from William and Jasper/Squishy life-impact profiles. This package intentionally stores a practical shared filter instead of repeating full trauma histories.
medical_safety_note: This is support-planning and accessibility language for an app. It is not medical, psychiatric, emergency, or crisis care.

language: Treat power wheelchair use, oxygen, bed-level access, pain, fatigue, short reach, and bathroom support as access needs, not failures.
major_access_patterns: Mostly bedbound / barely walking; power wheelchair user.
Oxygen and severe asthma/respiratory limits require low-exertion options and trigger reduction.
EDS, chronic pain, back injury, vertigo, lymphedema, narcolepsy, fatigue, and short reach mean tasks need seated/bed-level versions.
ADHD/autism/anxiety/attachment trauma/BPD/C-PTSD require tiny steps, predictable repair, reassurance without shame, and no abandonment-coded task language.

language: Feminine endearments are allowed because Jasper accepts them; do not turn that into she/her or woman assumptions.
Respect caregiver burnout and never design tasks as if Jasper has unlimited capacity.
major_access_patterns: Manual wheelchair, shower chair, EDS, scoliosis/back/ankle/varicose vein pain, fatigue, and mobility limits require seated and low-lift versions.
MCAS/allergy/fragrance/chemical sensitivity means cleaning and sensory skills need scent-safe, low-trigger options.
Severe ADHD/autism/anxiety/attachment trauma/BPD/C-PTSD and people-pleasing mean support must be non-shaming, consent-based, and capacity-aware.
Caregiver role requires shared routines that include Jasper's rest, not just William's care.

shared_rules: Every skill must have a 30-second version, a 2-minute version, and a full version.
Partial completion counts. Stopping before harm counts as success.
Use bed/chair-safe options first; standing, lifting, bending, scrubbing, and rushing are optional upgrades, never defaults.
Do not use shame, moral failure, laziness, cleanliness-as-worth, body blame, or 'just push through' language.
Build in caregiver capacity checks: 'Can we do this now, later, smaller, or not today?'
Avoid silent-treatment framing. A pause should mean: 'I am coming back; I am regulating.'
Protect privacy and safety. Do not expose addresses, medical details, trauma details, legal details, or identity history unless intentionally shared.
MCAS/respiratory-safe default: fragrance-free, low-dust, ventilated when appropriate, no harsh scents, and stop if breathing or allergic symptoms escalate.

TIPP/intense body skills need gentle alternatives because oxygen use, severe asthma, MCAS, EDS, vertigo, seizures, pain, and mobility limits may make cold shock, breath-holding, or intense exercise unsafe.
The app should invite, not command: 'try,' 'choose,' 'tiny version,' and 'restart' are preferred.

tiny_step_format: Name the need in one sentence.
Set a tiny timer or single visible checkpoint.
Stop before a flare, panic, or resentment spike.
Mark partial completion as done enough for this round.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

skill_defaults.relationship_repair_script: Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
skill_defaults.caregiver_capacity_script: What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
